q: Someone claims ChatGPT 'understands' their question. What's actually happening?

choices.text: It reads your mind and comprehends meaning like a human.
It looks up your question in a giant database of answers.
It predicts statistically likely next words based on patterns in training data.
It uses a sophisticated multi-layered logic engine that parses your grammar, identifies intent through semantic analysis, and routes your query to the appropriate knowledge module.

q: You run the exact same ChatGPT prompt twice and get different answers. Why?

choices.text: ChatGPT randomly picks from its database differently each time.
The model uses a temperature setting that introduces randomness into output generation.
It's a bug — AI should always give the same answer.
The servers are in different countries and use different data.

q: An AI image generator creates a realistic picture of a celebrity. Where did it learn to do that?

choices.text: It was manually programmed with that celebrity's face by developers.
It was trained on billions of images scraped from the internet, including photos of that person.
It searches Google Images in real time when you request a celebrity.
It combines random face features until it matches a name in its vocabulary.

q: A news headline reads: 'AI predicted the stock market with 94% accuracy.' What's your first thought?

choices.text: That's impressive — I should invest based on this AI.
I wonder what time period, dataset, and benchmark it was tested against.
Backtesting on historical data almost always overfits — the model learned past patterns, not future market behavior. 94% accuracy in-sample tells you nothing about real-world performance.
AI is getting too powerful if it can beat Wall Street.

q: Your friend says AI will replace ALL jobs within 5 years. How do you respond?

choices.text: Agree — we should all learn to live without working.
Some tasks will be automated, but most jobs will evolve rather than vanish entirely.
Disagree completely — AI can't do anything a human can do.

Current AI excels at pattern-based tasks like text generation and data analysis, but struggles with physical dexterity, multi-step causal reasoning, and novel social judgment — so displacement risk varies sharply by role type.

q: You're using ChatGPT to plan a complex project. Halfway through a long conversation, it starts contradicting its earlier recommendations. What's most likely happening?

choices.text: ChatGPT changed its mind as it 'learned' more from your conversation.
The conversation exceeded the model's context window, so it lost track of earlier details and is generating new responses without full context.
There's a server-side error — refresh the page and it will remember everything.
AI models periodically reset their reasoning mid-conversation to explore alternative approaches and give you a broader range of perspectives.

q: You upload a 200-page PDF to an AI chatbot and ask for a summary, but the output only covers the first 30 pages. What's the actual constraint?

choices.text: The AI is being lazy — use a more demanding prompt like 'summarize ALL pages thoroughly.'
The document exceeded the model's context window — the maximum amount of text it can process in a single pass.
PDF files aren't fully compatible with AI text processing — convert to plain text first and the problem will go away.
The AI's servers were overloaded at the time and timed out partway through — try again during off-peak hours when more computing resources are available for your request.

q: You asked AI to write a Python script yesterday and it worked perfectly. Today, the exact same prompt produces slightly different code that has a bug. What's the most likely explanation?

choices.text: Your subscription tier dropped and you're getting lower-quality outputs.
LLMs are non-deterministic — temperature settings introduce randomness, so identical prompts can produce different outputs each time.
Someone else's conversation corrupted the AI's shared memory between sessions.
The AI model was silently updated overnight and lost some of its ability to write Python code reliably — this happens regularly with cloud-based AI services.

q: You're building an app with AI help and paste your entire 2,000-line codebase into the chat for context. What's the main problem with this approach?

choices.text: AI can't read code — it only works well with natural language descriptions.
Large inputs may exceed token limits, and the model's attention degrades over long contexts — it may ignore or hallucinate details from sections it can't fully process.
It's fine — providing more context always produces better AI responses regardless of input length.
The main risk is that AI will store all your proprietary code and potentially expose it to other users who ask similar coding questions in the future.

q: You used AI to research a topic and it provided 10 academic citations with authors, journals, and publication years. When you check, 3 of the papers don't exist. This happened because...

choices.text: The AI's academic database is outdated and those papers were recently retracted from their journals.
LLMs generate citations by pattern-matching author names, journal formats, and topics from training data — they don't look up real papers, so fabricated references look completely authentic.
The AI copied citations from unreliable websites that had fake references listed.

You didn't explicitly request 'verified real citations only,' so the AI filled in gaps with plausible-sounding ones to complete your request — it's trying to be helpful by providing the number you asked for.

q: You used Midjourney to generate 20 logo variants and love one. You want to use it as your startup's official logo on all products and marketing. What's the critical concern?

choices.text: AI images are always free to use commercially — that's the whole point of generating them.
AI image generators output raster images, not scalable vector files — plus commercial rights depend on your subscription tier and the platform's terms of service.
The main concern is that AI logos look generic — you should add your company name on top and it's fine.
AI-generated logos are legally risky because the AI copies existing logos from its database, so you could get sued for trademark infringement.

q: You need to summarize a 50-page PDF report quickly. What's the best tool?

choices.text: Copy-paste sections into ChatGPT one at a time.
Upload the PDF directly to an LLM with file upload and prompt for a structured summary.
Ask a standard chatbot to summarize without uploading the file.
Use a dedicated PDF summarizer tool like Humata or Adobe AI Assistant, which are specifically designed for document analysis and can handle complex formatting, tables, and cross-references.

q: A teacher wants students to practice spoken Spanish conversations with AI. What's the best solution?

choices.text: Have students type back and forth with ChatGPT in Spanish.
Use a voice-enabled AI tutor like Speak, Duolingo AI, or ChatGPT voice mode.
Show students YouTube videos in Spanish.
Use Google Translate for pronunciation practice.

q: You need to send personalized birthday emails to 500 clients automatically. Best approach?

choices.text: Write one email in ChatGPT and copy-paste it 500 times with edits.
Use an email platform like Mailchimp with merge tags, A/B testing on subject lines, segmented scheduling based on time zones, and automated follow-up sequences for non-openers.
Use a CRM with AI personalization integrated into an automation workflow.
Ask ChatGPT to generate all 500 emails and send them manually.

q: You want to build a simple website but have zero coding experience. Best path?

choices.text: Ask ChatGPT to write the HTML and paste it somewhere.
Hire a web developer.
Use a no-code AI site builder like Framer, Wix ADI, or Squarespace AI.
Use a vibe-coding tool like Bolt or Lovable with AI prompting, then deploy on Netlify.

q: You ask ChatGPT to convert messy addresses into structured data, but it keeps misplacing city names in the street field and mixing up states and zip codes. Being very specific about field definitions in plain English didn't fix it. An experienced AI user would fix this by...

choices.text: Switching to a more powerful model — GPT-4 handles structured data much better than GPT-3.5.

Providing 3-4 few-shot examples showing exact input→output mappings, specifying the output schema with field names, and requesting JSON format. Few-shot examples anchor the model's parsing pattern far more reliably than verbal instructions alone.

Writing a very detailed prompt explaining every field: 'Street means the house number and street name, City means the municipality...' with thorough definitions for each field and explicit rules about what goes where.
Adding 'You are an expert data parser with 20 years of experience' — role-based prompting activates specialized knowledge for data extraction tasks.

q: You need AI to extract data from text and return it in a specific table format, but every response uses slightly different formatting. What's the most reliable fix?

choices.text: Keep regenerating until you get the format you want — eventually it will match.
Provide an explicit output template with a concrete example of the exact format, and use lower temperature settings for more deterministic output.
Tell the AI to 'please be consistent with formatting' — explicit instructions about consistency solve most formatting issues.
Always request JSON output — structured data formats like JSON are inherently more consistent and reliable across repeated runs than free-form text or markdown tables.

q: Your coworker thinks there's an edge case bug in your AI-generated validation function. You paste the code into a new chat and say: 'My coworker found a bug with empty strings in this function.' The AI immediately says 'Your coworker is right! Here's the fix...' and rewrites the logic. But you test both versions — the original actually handled empty strings correctly. What happened?

choices.text: The AI found a genuine improvement your testing missed — edge cases are notoriously tricky and the AI's version is probably more robust even if both pass your tests.

LLMs exhibit 'sycophancy' — when you frame something as a bug ('my coworker found a bug'), the model treats that as an instruction to find and fix one, even fabricating a problem in correct code. Your framing predetermined the response.

The AI wasn't being sycophantic — it was just confused by ambiguous phrasing. Rephrase as a neutral question: 'Does this function handle empty strings correctly?' and it will give the accurate answer.
The AI has more comprehensive testing capabilities than manual tests, so there may actually be an obscure edge case that your tests didn't cover.

q: You've been going back and forth with AI for 15 messages trying to fix a sorting algorithm. Each time you describe the bug clearly, it apologizes and produces a 'fix' that has the SAME logical error in a slightly different form. What's the most effective next step?

choices.text: Keep iterating — describe the error even more precisely with a step-by-step trace showing exactly where the logic goes wrong.

Start a fresh conversation. Paste only the requirements and the specific failing test case. Long conversations accumulate context noise — the model weights its own failed attempts as heavily as your corrections, reinforcing the error pattern.
Try a completely different AI model — some models are better at algorithms than others.
The AI clearly can't solve this — write the algorithm manually instead.

q: You ask AI to analyze a dataset and it gives you a confident but wrong conclusion. You point out the error with evidence, and it apologizes but gives a DIFFERENT wrong conclusion. What's the best approach?

choices.text: Provide even more data and context so the AI has everything it needs to reach the correct answer this time.

Ask it to 'think step by step' — break the analysis into explicit stages where you verify each intermediate result before it proceeds. LLMs make fewer reasoning errors when forced to show their work rather than jumping to conclusions.
AI clearly can't handle this type of analysis — export the data to Excel or a statistics tool instead.
Ask the AI to first list all its assumptions about the data, then perform the analysis — this way you can catch where its reasoning diverges from reality before it reaches a conclusion.

q: AI wrote a research summary with claims you want to verify. You ask it to 'cite your sources.' It responds with 5 academic paper titles, author names, journal names, and DOI numbers. What should you conclude?

choices.text: The citations prove the content is well-researched — AI wouldn't fabricate full academic references complete with DOI numbers.
Asking for sources was a smart verification step. Now spot-check one or two of the citations to confirm they're real.

AI generates fake citations — complete with realistic authors, journals, and DOI numbers — just as fluently as it generates any other text. Asking for sources provided zero verification. Check every claim against Google Scholar independently.

DOIs are standardized unique identifiers managed by the International DOI Foundation, so they're extremely difficult to randomly fabricate — if the DOI format looks valid and structured, the papers almost certainly exist.

q: You're unsure about an AI's factual claim, so you open 3 new chat sessions with the same model and ask the identical question. All 3 give the same answer. What can you conclude?

choices.text: Three separate sessions all confirming the same answer is strong evidence — like polling three independent experts who all agree.
The consistency strongly suggests accuracy. It's worth one external check to be thorough, but three matching responses is a very positive sign.

Nothing — the same model with the same training data and weights will tend to produce similar outputs. Consistency from one model is not independent verification. Only external, non-AI sources can actually confirm the claim.

If all three responses are nearly word-for-word identical, it means the AI is retrieving from a cached or memorized answer rather than generating, which is actually more likely to be a verified fact from its training data.

q: An employee pastes confidential company financial data into the free version of ChatGPT to help draft a report. The CTO is furious. What's the specific technical risk?

choices.text: ChatGPT will sell the data directly to competitors.
On default consumer settings, conversations may be used for model training — proprietary data could influence future model outputs visible to other users.
The CTO is overreacting — ChatGPT automatically deletes all conversation data after each session ends.
The main risk is that ChatGPT might misinterpret the financial figures and produce an inaccurate report that gets shared externally.

q: An AI coding assistant suggests installing a package called 'python-dateutils2' to fix your date parsing bug. You've used 'python-dateutil' before but never heard of this variant. What should concern you?

choices.text: It's probably a newer, updated version of the package you already know — install it and try.
AI frequently hallucinates package names. Installing unverified packages risks typosquatting attacks — malicious packages published under names similar to popular ones.
Just install it — if it doesn't work, you can always uninstall it with no harm done.
Search for the package documentation and GitHub repository first to verify it exists, has active maintainers, and does what you need before adding it to your project.

q: ChatGPT gave you a detailed legal analysis for a contract dispute. To verify, you paste the analysis into Claude and ask 'Is this legal analysis correct?' Claude responds that it's 'largely accurate with minor clarifications.' How reliable is this cross-verification?

choices.text: Very reliable — two different AI models built by different companies independently agree, like getting a second doctor's opinion from a different hospital.
Reasonably reliable — Claude provided its own corrections rather than just agreeing, which shows genuine independent analysis. Double-check the specific points Claude flagged and you should be fine.

Weak — LLMs share overlapping training data and similar tendencies to generate plausible-sounding legal language. One AI reviewing another tends to agree because both optimize for plausibility, not legal accuracy. Only a human lawyer or primary legal sources can truly verify.

Somewhat reliable — since Claude and ChatGPT use completely different architectures, training datasets, and reasoning approaches, their agreement carries more weight than asking the same model twice, though you'd still want a human check for anything legally binding.

q: Your company uses ChatGPT Team plan for customer support. An employee switches to the free tier for a complex query. What's the specific risk?

choices.text: The free tier gives worse answers than the paid version.
Free-tier conversations may be used for model training, so customer PII could leak into future model outputs.
The employee might exceed the free tier's usage limits and get locked out in the middle of helping a customer.

Using the free tier violates the company's software licensing agreement, creates an audit trail gap, and could trigger compliance violations under the company's data governance framework if discovered.

q: You ask AI about drug interactions for a medication you're taking. It begins with: 'I'm not a medical professional and this isn't medical advice. Please consult your doctor.' Then provides detailed interaction information. How should you interpret the disclaimer?

choices.text: The AI is being transparent about its limitations — the information that follows is likely reliable since the system is being honest and cautious rather than overconfidently presenting itself as an authority.

The disclaimer is a trained response pattern baked in by developers, not genuine self-awareness. It has zero correlation with the accuracy of what follows — AI generates disclaimers and hallucinated medical facts with equal fluency.

Any AI that includes medical disclaimers has gone through responsible safety training, which means its health-related responses have been validated against medical databases and reviewed by professionals during the development process.

The disclaimer shows the AI recognizes this is a high-stakes topic, so it's likely being extra careful and conservative with the specific information — treat it as a reliable starting point but verify critical details with a pharmacist.

q: You ask AI to create a competitive analysis comparing pricing of 5 SaaS tools. It responds: 'I don't have access to real-time pricing data and cannot provide accurate comparisons.' What should you do?

choices.text: The AI was honest about its limitations. Research the pricing manually or hire an analyst — this task genuinely requires real-time data that AI doesn't have access to.

Rephrase the task: provide the pricing data yourself and ask AI to format the comparison table, generate the analysis framework, or write the narrative. Many AI refusals are overly cautious guardrails, not hard technical limitations — restructure the task around what AI CAN do.

Accept the refusal — AI should never be used for tasks involving current market data, since its training data is always outdated and it fundamentally cannot access real-time information under any circumstances.

Switch to a different AI model that has web browsing or search capabilities — the problem is simply that this particular model lacks internet access, which is the only barrier to completing this task.

q: You ask two AI chatbots about a historical trade agreement. Chatbot A responds with specific dates (March 14, 1987), named officials (Deputy Minister Kowalski), vote counts (217-198), and a direct quote. Chatbot B gives a general overview without specific details. Which response should you trust more?

choices.text: Chatbot A — the specific dates, names, and vote counts indicate deeper knowledge. Anyone can give a vague overview, but specific facts require real information.

Chatbot A is more trustworthy because specific, verifiable claims are inherently more accountable — the AI is putting forward concrete facts that can be checked, which shows confidence in their accuracy.

Neither is inherently more trustworthy. LLMs generate convincing specifics — exact dates, official names, vote tallies, direct quotes — as easily as vague summaries. Specific-sounding fabrications are MORE dangerous because they look authoritative. Verify both against primary sources.
Chatbot B is actually more trustworthy — an AI that gives vaguer answers may be showing appropriate uncertainty, while Chatbot A's excessive specificity is a red flag for overconfident confabulation.

q: Your company's AI chatbot occasionally includes specific details from other customers' previous conversations in its responses. What's the most likely technical cause?

choices.text: A hacker breached the system and is deliberately leaking customer data.
The model was fine-tuned on or retrieves from a shared data store that doesn't properly isolate per-customer data, causing cross-contamination of context between sessions.
The AI copies information from the public internet where customers may have posted their own data on review sites or social media.
This is a known limitation of large language models — they sometimes retain fragments of previous conversations in memory and surface them unpredictably in later sessions with different users.

q: You prompt ChatGPT: 'Our email campaign had a 2% click rate, which seems low. Analyze why it failed.' It gives 5 detailed reasons for failure. You open a new chat and prompt: 'Our email campaign had a 2% click rate, which is solid for our industry. Analyze why it succeeded.' It gives 5 equally convincing reasons for success. What does this tell you about using AI for analysis?

choices.text: The AI correctly identified that 2% can be interpreted both ways depending on context — both analyses are valid and show AI's ability to consider multiple perspectives.
The second analysis was more accurate because you gave it better context about industry benchmarks, helping it understand the data correctly.

LLMs generate plausible arguments for whatever framing you provide. Neither response independently evaluated whether 2% is good or bad — both just followed your lead. You need to provide raw data with industry benchmarks and ask for objective assessment without pre-framing the conclusion.
This shows AI can see multiple perspectives — use both analyses together for a balanced, comprehensive view of the campaign's performance.

q: You're debugging AI-generated code and discover it calls an API endpoint that was deprecated and removed 6 months ago. This happened because...

choices.text: The AI intentionally chose the older API because it was more stable and better documented.
The model's training data has a knowledge cutoff — it doesn't know about changes, deprecations, or new APIs released after that date.
You should have specified which API version to use in your prompt — the AI defaulted to whatever version appeared most frequently in its training data and online documentation.
The AI searched for the API with the most online documentation and tutorials available and selected the most referenced version.

q: You ask AI to write a recursive function. The code looks clean, but you notice there's no base case — it would recurse infinitely. You ask: 'Does this function have proper termination conditions?' The AI responds: 'Yes, the recursive call on line 4 naturally terminates when the input reaches zero.' But line 4 has no such check. Why did AI respond this way?

choices.text: There's a subtle implicit termination condition in the recursion pattern that you're missing — read line 4 more carefully.
The AI made a mistake but is 'doubling down' because it doesn't want to admit errors — rephrase your question more clearly.

LLMs don't execute or trace code — they generate text that sounds like a code review. When asked 'does this work?', they produce a confident explanation by pattern-matching what a code review should say, not by actually verifying logic. Always run the code yourself.
The AI internally tested the code and found it works in most cases. The missing base case only matters for certain inputs, so the AI's assessment is partially correct.

q: You need AI to help analyze a customer dataset that contains names, emails, and purchase history. What's the correct workflow?

choices.text: Paste it directly into the chat — AI processes data in real time and doesn't permanently store anything.
Strip or pseudonymize all PII before sending to AI. Use enterprise/API tiers with data processing agreements rather than consumer chatbots for sensitive data.
Add a disclaimer in the prompt: 'This data is confidential — do not store or share it.' AI must follow user instructions about data handling.
Remove customer names but keep emails and purchase data intact — the analysis needs unique identifiers to detect patterns, and emails without names aren't really personally identifiable information.

q: You've been iterating with AI on a complex coding task for 40+ messages. The responses are getting repetitive, less relevant, and sometimes contradict earlier solutions. What's happening?

choices.text: The AI is getting 'tired' from processing too many requests in a row.
As conversations grow, they fill the context window. The model loses track of earlier constraints and its attention degrades — start a new conversation with a focused summary of where you left off.
You've exceeded your daily quality quota and the system quietly switched you to a weaker, cheaper model to save resources.
The AI has exhausted its knowledge on this particular topic and is now recycling and paraphrasing its previous answers because it has nothing new to contribute.

q: You want to build a personal portfolio website using a vibe-coding tool like Bolt.new. What produces the best results?

choices.text: Open Bolt and type 'make me a portfolio website' — AI works best with short, simple prompts that give it creative freedom.
Sketch what pages you need and what content goes on each before prompting.

Write a detailed spec: list each page with sections, specify color scheme and fonts, include example copy, and attach a screenshot of a design you like. Vibe-coding tools generate complete apps in one shot — front-loading detail in your first prompt avoids frustrating multi-round revision cycles.
Ask AI which tech stack is best for portfolio sites before building anything.

q: You built a web app with AI. Desktop looks perfect, but on mobile the layout is completely broken — text overflows, buttons overlap, sidebar covers content. This is extremely common with AI-generated web code. Why?

choices.text: AI can only generate code for one screen size at a time — you need to specifically request a separate mobile version.

LLMs are trained on more desktop-oriented code examples, so AI-generated layouts default to fixed widths and absolute positioning. You must specifically request mobile-first responsive design with viewport-relative units in your initial prompt.
Mobile browsers render CSS differently than desktop browsers — this is a browser compatibility issue that would happen with human-written code too.
The AI probably used an outdated CSS technique. Describe the specific layout issues on mobile, paste the CSS, and ask for a responsive fix using modern flexbox or grid.

q: You vibe-coded a working web app that uses the OpenAI API. It works on your laptop. You deploy it to a public website. Within hours, you get a $200 bill from OpenAI. What went wrong?

choices.text: OpenAI charges more for production websites than for development testing.
Your API key was in the client-side JavaScript code, so anyone who visited your site could see it and make unlimited API calls at your expense.
The hosting provider added extra charges for API traffic going through their servers.
AI APIs are expensive — you should have used a free alternative like Hugging Face.

q: Your AI-built app works but loads very slowly. What's the best approach?

choices.text: Ask AI to 'make the app faster' without any other context.
Upgrade to a more expensive hosting plan.
Profile what's slow in DevTools, then ask AI to fix the specific bottleneck.

Run a full Lighthouse audit, analyze the waterfall chart in the Network tab, check for render-blocking resources, measure Time to First Byte, and create a comprehensive performance report before making any changes.

q: You've been vibe-coding with AI for a month. What's the MOST surprising lesson experienced AI builders learn that beginners don't expect?

choices.text: AI is faster than expected — most apps can be built in a single conversation with a good enough prompt.

AI-generated code accumulates invisible technical debt — it works but uses inconsistent patterns, redundant logic, and hardcoded values across files. Learning to read and refactor AI output matters more than prompting skill.
Knowing enough to direct AI precisely, debug its output, and own the final product is the most important meta-skill.
The biggest differentiator is knowing which AI tool to use for each task — Claude for analysis, GPT for creative writing, Copilot for code.

q: Your company wants to build a customer support AI that answers questions about your product. What's the most important technical decision?

choices.text: Choose the most expensive AI model — bigger is always better.
Fine-tune a large language model on your entire customer database, support tickets, internal docs, and product manuals so it learns everything about your company from scratch.
Use RAG — connect the model to your knowledge base so it retrieves relevant docs before answering.
Train a custom model from scratch on your company data.

q: A company claims their LLM scores 95% on a popular benchmark. You later discover that text from this benchmark appeared in the model's training data. Why is this a critical problem?

choices.text: It's not a problem — more training data always improves the model, and benchmark text is high-quality data worth including.

This is benchmark contamination: the model likely memorized specific input-output pairs from the test set. When LLMs encounter familiar sequences, they reproduce them from memory rather than reasoning — so 95% measures recall, not capability.
The bigger issue is that benchmarks are too easy — the company should create harder tests to challenge the model.
It's concerning but not critical — a well-designed model will generalize from any training data, so exposure to benchmark examples shouldn't significantly inflate scores if the architecture is sound.

q: You need AI to extract structured data from messy customer reviews (sentiment, product mentioned, issue type). What's the most reliable approach?

choices.text: Ask AI to 'analyze these reviews' and hope it figures out the structure.
Provide a schema with exact field names, give 2-3 examples of input→output, and specify the output format (JSON).

Write an extremely detailed 500-word system prompt explaining every possible edge case, sentiment nuance, product category taxonomy, and issue classification hierarchy with decision trees for ambiguous cases.
Process one review at a time with a simple prompt for each field.

q: You're building an AI app that needs to remember context from previous conversations with each user. What architecture do you need?

choices.text: Just use a smarter AI model — it will remember everything.
Store conversation history in a database and include relevant past messages in each new prompt's context window.

Implement a comprehensive memory management system with vector embeddings for semantic search across all past conversations, automatic summarization of older interactions, tiered storage with hot/warm/cold memory layers, and periodic memory consolidation jobs.
Save the full chat log and paste it all into each new request.

q: Your AI-built web app works perfectly during development. After launch, users report random crashes, garbled text, and incorrect calculations. You check the code and the logic LOOKS fine. What's the most likely cause specific to AI-generated code?

choices.text: The code needs more computational resources — production traffic requires better servers than your local development machine.

AI-generated code is 'happy path' code — it works with the clean examples you tested but omits input validation, error handling, and edge case logic for nulls, Unicode, empty strings, and unexpected formats that real users inevitably produce.
You tested with clean sample data but real-world data is messier — add more input validation and error handling to handle unexpected formats.
The deployment environment has different library versions than your development setup, causing subtle behavior differences.
